one_line_takeaway: Steering a small set of SAE-identified 'expert' features in intermediate ViT layers can restore near-original forget-class accuracy for most approximate unlearning methods (and even for full retraining from a pretrained checkpoint), showing that low output-based forget-set accuracy often reflects suppression rather than true representation-level deletion.
key_finding: Most unlearning methods that manipulate outputs or loss functions (e.g., AdvNegGrad, RandomLabel, SCRUB) or perform simple parameter tweaks (Finetune, SalUn, UNSIR, L1-Sparse) achieve near-0% forget-class accuracy after unlearning but can be driven back to near-original accuracy purely by steering a small set of SAE-identified features at deeper layers -- e.g., on CIFAR-10, AdvNegGrad and RandomLabel go from 0.00% to 100.00% restored accuracy at layer 10, SCRUB goes from 6.00% to 100.00%, and even full Retrain (retraining from the pretrained checkpoint on the retain set only) goes from 0.00% to 99.60% (layer 9) / 99.40% (layer 10) restored accuracy on CIFAR-10 and to 98.97% at layer 10 on ImageNette. In contrast, methods that structurally modify intermediate-layer parameters show much lower restorability: EU-K (layer reset) stays at 0.00% restored accuracy across all layers on both datasets, and weight-dampening methods Bad-T, CF-K, and SSD show comparatively small or even negative restoration deltas. Restoration also peaks at different layer depths depending on dataset complexity: layers 8-9 for the simpler CIFAR-10 versus layers 9-10 for the more complex ImageNette, which the authors interpret as dataset-complexity-dependent 'semantic bottleneck' layers.
core_contribution: The paper's own novel contribution is a 'restoration-based' representation-level analysis framework for auditing machine unlearning methods. It (1) uses Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) trained on intermediate-layer activations of a ViT-B/16 to identify 'class-specific expert features' via an F1-based precision/recall scoring of feature activations, aligned across original and unlearned models with the Hungarian algorithm; and (2) performs 'selective restoration' by steering the unlearned model's activations at these expert-feature indices toward the original model's values (h_hat[j] = h_unl[j] + alpha*(h_orig[j]-h_unl[j]), alpha=10) and propagating the steered representation through the remaining layers. A rise in forget-class accuracy after this steering is interpreted as evidence that the unlearning method only suppressed (masked at output) rather than deleted (removed from intermediate representations) the target information. The authors apply this framework to 12 unlearning methods on two image-classification datasets and derive design/evaluation guidelines from the results.
threat_model: Not an adversarial attacker/defender threat model in the classic security sense; the paper frames the risk as an auditing/compliance problem. Threat scenario: pretrained models are shared/redistributed on platforms such as Hugging Face after supposedly 'unlearning' sensitive, copyrighted, or private data (e.g., to satisfy GDPR's right to be forgotten). Output-based unlearning metrics (forget-set accuracy, membership inference attacks) can be satisfied even when the underlying semantic representations of the 'forgotten' class remain encoded in intermediate layers. Anyone with white-box access to the redistributed model's activations (the paper's own steering procedure) can recover the ostensibly deleted class information, i.e., a downstream party could exploit persistent representations despite a model appearing to have forgotten the data by output metrics alone.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) Generalizability -- analysis is restricted to Vision Transformers on image classification and class-wise unlearning; other architectures may distribute information differently, and extending to instance-wise unlearning or to LLMs/generative models is left to future work. (2) SAE-based interpretation caveats -- identified features depend on SAE hyperparameters (sparsity level, expansion factor) and may not fully capture the model's internal behavior; the paper cites Heap et al. 2025 showing SAEs can extract seemingly meaningful features even from randomly initialized (untrained) models, which the authors flag as a reason for caution about their own feature-selection pipeline and a motivation for alternative interpretability methods.

quantitative_results: Feature-ablation validation (Table 1): zeroing expert features drops CIFAR-10 class-2 forget accuracy by -95.00 (layer 8), -98.60 (layer 9), -99.00 (layer 10) points while retain accuracy changes by only +0.10/+0.18/+0.24; ImageNette class-7 forget accuracy drops by -82.80/-96.44/-98.80 while retain accuracy changes by +0.50/+0.52/+0.52
